prompt:
On the first Saturday of August, I showed up at the Rialto Theater with work gloves and the idea that I would spend three quiet hours scraping paint. The marquee had been dark for years, and in daylight its metal letters looked thinner than I expected, as if a hard rain might wash them away. Mr. Soto, who had once sold tickets there as a teenager, handed me a scraper and climbed the ladder beside me. He worked slowly, pausing whenever he recognized the title of an old film still faintly visible beneath newer layers of paint. With each name, he attached a memory: a line that wrapped around the block, a winter night when the furnace failed but nobody left, an afternoon matinee interrupted by applause before the credits had even finished. By the time we stopped for water, I had cleared only a narrow strip of metal. Yet the marquee no longer seemed delicate. Its dents and rust now looked less like damage than evidence that many hands had repaired it so it could keep facing the street. When the volunteer coordinator asked whether I could return the following week, I said yes before I thought to check my calendar. Walking home, I passed the theater poster case. The empty frame in its glass door looked neat, but suddenly it also looked wrong.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The narrator discovers that theater restoration is easier than expected.
choices.B: Mr. Soto persuades the narrator to become interested in classic films.
choices.C: Hearing Mr. Soto’s memories changes the narrator’s understanding of the old theater.
choices.D: The narrator decides that the theater should become a museum instead of reopening.

explanation: The narrator begins expecting only routine work and ends seeing the theater differently because Mr. Soto’s stories make the marquee feel meaningful.

choices.A: repaired the theater’s furnace during a winter screening
choices.B: sold tickets at the theater as a teenager
choices.C: owned the theater before it closed
choices.D: painted the movie titles on the marquee

explanation: The passage states this directly in the first paragraph when introducing Mr. Soto.

prompt:
Urban planners often assume that if a public staircase is visible and well maintained, people will naturally use it instead of an escalator. A recent study of train stations suggests that visibility is only part of the story. In several stations, researchers added large signs showing how long it would take to reach the platforms by stairs and placed simple artwork along the stair walls. In similar stations nearby, the stairs remained clean and visible but otherwise unchanged.
The researchers found that stair use increased most in the stations with both travel-time signs and artwork. Riders who chose the stairs were later more likely to remember something about the route: a mosaic pattern, a local history fact printed beside a landing, or the number of steps between floors. In the unchanged stations, most riders who took the stairs did so quickly and rarely recalled any detail from the trip.
The study does not claim that decoration alone can reshape commuter behavior. Convenience still matters, especially when trains are arriving soon. Instead, the researchers argue that people are more likely to choose an active route when it feels both manageable and mildly engaging. In that sense, the staircase becomes not just a piece of infrastructure but a short experience.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Clean staircases are always used more often than escalators.
choices.B: Travel-time signs and small design details can make commuters more likely to choose and remember a staircase route.
choices.C: Artwork is more important than convenience in all train stations.
choices.D: Most commuters dislike escalators because they are too crowded.

explanation: The study found the biggest increase in stair use where both signs and artwork were added, and those riders later remembered more details.

explanation: The first paragraph states directly that these were added in several of the stations.

prompt:
Some readers treat footnotes as interruptions, little fences built across the bottom of a page. In some books, that judgment is fair. A note that merely repeats a date or translates a phrase any attentive reader could already understand can feel like an unnecessary tug at the sleeve. Yet in essays and histories, the best footnotes do something subtler. They do not pull the reader away from the main argument so much as widen the ground beneath it. A note may quietly show where a claim came from, acknowledge a disagreement the author chose not to pursue in the main text, or point toward a story too interesting to disappear completely.
That extra space matters because good prose depends partly on pressure. A historian cannot pause every paragraph to include every qualification, side path, or counterexample without slowing the main narrative to a crawl. Footnotes solve this problem by creating a second register of writing: one that can be patient where the main text must be swift. They let authors remain concise without pretending that concision has no cost.
I once watched a student compare two editions of the same historical essay. In one, the notes had been removed for a general audience. The argument remained clear, but it felt oddly flatter, as if the floor beneath it had been sanded smooth. In the annotated edition, a brief note about an alternate witness account did not overturn the author’s conclusion. It simply reminded the reader that the conclusion had been built among competing possibilities. That reminder made the essay feel not weaker, but more trustworthy.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Footnotes usually distract readers and should be removed from most books.
choices.B: Useful footnotes can strengthen a text by adding support, complexity, and transparency without overloading the main prose.
choices.C: Historical essays are more persuasive when they avoid acknowledging disagreement.
choices.D: General readers prefer essays with longer main paragraphs and fewer notes.

explanation: The passage argues that the best notes widen and support the argument while allowing the main text to stay concise.

choices.D: a note that points toward an interesting side story

explanation: The author explicitly says that such notes can feel like “an unnecessary tug at the sleeve.”

Question:

choices.A: make the page physically longer
choices.B: give the main argument additional support and scope
choices.C: distract the reader from the author’s central point
choices.D: replace the main argument with several smaller ones

explanation: The metaphor suggests that notes help support the argument and broaden the context around it.

prompt:
Some readers treat footnotes as interruptions, little fences built across the bottom of a page. In some books, that judgment is fair. A note that merely repeats a date or translates a phrase any attentive reader could already understand can feel like an unnecessary tug at the sleeve. Yet in essays and histories, the best footnotes do something subtler. They do not pull the reader away from the main argument so much as widen the ground beneath it. A note may quietly show where a claim came from, acknowledge a disagreement the author chose not to pursue in the main text, or point toward a story too interesting to disappear completely.
That extra space matters because good prose depends partly on pressure. A historian cannot pause every paragraph to include every qualification, side path, or counterexample without slowing the main narrative to a crawl. Footnotes solve this problem by creating a second register of writing: one that can be patient where the main text must be swift. They let authors remain concise without pretending that concision has no cost.
I once watched a student compare two editions of the same historical essay. In one, the notes had been removed for a general audience. The argument remained clear, but it felt oddly flatter, as if the floor beneath it had been sanded smooth. In the annotated edition, a brief note about an alternate witness account did not overturn the author’s conclusion. It simply reminded the reader that the conclusion had been built among competing possibilities. That reminder made the essay feel not weaker, but more trustworthy.
Question:
The second paragraph mainly serves to:

choices.A: explain why footnotes can solve a problem of pace and compression in the main text
choices.B: argue that historians should write more slowly and less clearly
choices.C: suggest that footnotes are useful only in scientific writing
choices.D: prove that concise writing is always superior to detailed writing

explanation: The second paragraph argues that notes create a second register where details and qualifications can appear without slowing the main narrative.

choices.A: show that general readers dislike all annotated texts

Question:
The author would most likely agree with which statement?

choices.A: Footnotes are useful only when they are longer than the main text.
choices.B: Concise prose should avoid all qualifications in order to remain readable.
choices.C: A text can become more trustworthy when it acknowledges complexity in notes.
choices.D: Notes should always be removed for general audiences.

explanation: The author says the note made the essay feel “not weaker, but more trustworthy.”

Question:
Which choice best describes the structure of the passage?

choices.A: It rejects footnotes entirely and then explains how readers can avoid them.
choices.B: It begins with a common complaint, refines the role of good footnotes, and ends with an example.
choices.C: It compares several historians and ranks them by writing style.
choices.D: It describes a classroom argument and then shifts to a biography of the author.

explanation: This matches the passage’s progression from objection to argument to illustrative anecdote.

prompt:
For decades, many stream managers removed fallen logs from channels as quickly as possible. The reasoning seemed obvious: wood obstructed the flow of water. More recent field studies suggest that this view is incomplete. In forested streams, submerged logs can slow fast water, trap gravel, and create deeper pools where fish shelter during dry periods. The same pieces of wood can also redirect current toward the center of a channel, reducing erosion along one bank while increasing it in another place.
To test how wood affects stream conditions, researchers compared reaches where large logs had been left in place with reaches nearby where similar logs had been removed years earlier. The reaches with wood contained more deep pools and more patches of stored gravel. Juvenile fish were also observed there more often during late summer, when shallow riffles warmed quickly.
The researchers do not argue that every log should remain wherever it falls. In places near bridges or culverts, wood can create risks during floods. Still, the study suggests that treating all fallen wood as mere blockage can oversimplify the role it plays in stream structure and habitat.
Question:
Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Fallen logs always increase flood risk and should be removed from all streams.
choices.B: Wood in streams can shape habitat and channel conditions, so removing it automatically may be too simple a policy.
choices.C: Juvenile fish survive only in streams with bridges and culverts.
choices.D: Gravel storage matters more than water flow in every stream system.

explanation: The passage argues that the old view of wood as mere obstruction misses its ecological and structural roles.

Question:
Which choice best describes the structure of the passage?

choices.A: It begins with an older view, presents comparative evidence, and ends with a qualified conclusion.
choices.B: It tells the history of one stream restoration project and then rejects scientific evidence.
